fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

This matter involves the applicant wife, Ms Poole, born in 1981 (“the wife”) and the respondent husband, Mr Bray, born in 1979 (“the husband”). During the final hearing the parties were able to settle matters in respect of the parenting arrangements, however, the property matters remain outstanding for determination.
The significant issues that the Court must determine are:
(a) whether a financial agreement entered into between the parties said to be pursuant to s 90B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) ought to be set aside;

(b) the pool of assets in circumstances where the husband contends that property held in his name is held either wholly or partially on trust for third parties; and

(c) the treatment of a debt owed by the husband to the ATO in the sum of $574,963.38;

BACKGROUND

The parties commenced a relationship in 2009 and were married in 2010. The parties entered into a financial agreement purportedly pursuant to s 90B of the Act on 26 August 2010 (“the financial agreement”).[1] The wife now seeks for the financial agreement to be set aside and for an adjustment of the parties’ property interests.
There are four children to the marriage: [2]
(a) W born in 2013 aged 13 years;

(b) X born in 2014 aged 11 years;

(c) Y born in 2016 aged 9 years; and

(d) Z born in 2018 aged 7 years.

The two middle children, X and Y, have been diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder. Y has also been diagnosed with ADHD and has a severe learning disability, although the wife says that a severe learning disability is not a recognised diagnosis in Australia and is generally stated by the therapist. Y has recently been diagnosed with epilepsy. The two children receive NDIS funding.[3]
When the parties married, the wife was employed as an educator.[4] She took maternity leave in 2013 and returned in 2017 and was then on a part-time basis of one day per week. She ceased work in 2018 when Z was born. She returned in early 2020 on a full-time basis.[5]
The husband completed a Bachelor Degree in 2003. He commenced employment at U Pty Ltd as a clerk in 2004. The husband had several promotions until becoming a finance professional earning approximately $110,000 per annum.[6]
In 2011, the husband began operating a small business as a sideline which specialised in renovation work. He says that he undertook handyman jobs allowing him to acquire additional funds and the necessary skills to renovate the parties’ investment properties.[7]
In July 2016, the husband was made redundant from U Pty Ltd and decided not to find new employment, instead he gives evidence that he prioritised providing care for the children whilst continuing his handyman business and renovation of the parties’ properties.[8] The husband has recently commenced an apprenticeship earning approximately $908 weekly before tax.[9]
The wife states that throughout the relationship her primary duty was to care for the children and the household whilst the husband was the ‘breadwinner’. She agrees that the husband worked hard, including working six days per week (only taking Sunday off). She states that she was responsible for taking care of the bills and school fees whilst the husband invested his earnings.[10]
The wife asserts that the relationship was characterised by family violence perpetrated on her and the children by the husband. She alleges that the husband would consume drugs and alcohol which would increase his abusive behaviour.[11] She states that the husband’s drinking, anger and aggression existed from the commencement of the relationship and escalated towards the end of the relationship.[12] This is denied by the husband.
The parties separated in mid-2022 when the husband left the matrimonial home after being served with an Interim Intervention Order obtained by the wife naming her and the children as protected persons.[13] The husband says that the separation and the IVO came as a surprise to him.[14] Following separation the wife has been the primary carer of the children.
The final parenting orders made by consent on the third day of the final hearing provide for the wife to have sole parental responsibility, for the children to live with her and for the husband to spend limited time with the children with various conditions.

Procedural History

The wife commenced these proceedings on 22 July 2022 by way of filing an Initiating Application after she says the husband, unbeknownst to her, had placed the property at D Street, Suburb C (“D Street property”) for sale.
The wife states that there was also an active PEXA workspace for various properties. First, for the sale of a property at H Street, Suburb J property (“H Street property”) to Mr K and a trust where the husband’s sister, Ms O, husband was the sole director of the trustee company. Further, an active PEXA workspace for the properties at 2 L Street, Suburb N (“2 L Street property”) and P Street, Suburb Q (“P Street property”). The proposed purchaser of the P Street property was Ms O who would be retaining her interests in the property and a company which Ms O was sole director of. The wife states that she lodged caveats on these three properties on 5 August 2022.[15] A property at 3 L Street, Suburb N (“3 L Street property”) had already been sold at this time.
On 9 August 2022, Judge Burt made ex parte orders for the husband to pay the wife’s solicitors all funds either paid or payable to him in respect of those properties and he was restrained from selling or encumbering further properties.
On 22 February 2023, SJR Conlan (as she then was) made orders for:
(a) discovery;

(b) the sale of the property at G Street, Suburb C (“G Street property”);

(c) for the husband to meet all arrears in respect of any mortgage, rates and taxes in respect of the properties and to continue paying those costs aside from the property at E Street, Suburb F (“E Street property”); and

(d) for neither party to encumber, or further encumber, the properties without the consent of the other party.

Between June 2023 until October 2024, the parties filed nine applications in this Court. These ranged from Applications in a Proceeding, a Contravention Application and an Application for Review.
The husband filed an Application in a Proceeding on the first day of the final hearing where he asserted that the wife contravened SJR Conlan’s orders of 22 February 2023 by entering into an agreement with T Pty Ltd who lodged a caveat against the E Street property which she failed to disclose.[16] The husband sought an order by way of that application that, amongst other things, the wife is restrained from dealing with the funds received from T Pty Ltd. On that day, those orders were varied and the application was otherwise dismissed.

PROPOSALS

The wife seeks that the financial agreement be set aside. She seeks the following orders on a final basis which represent, on her case, a 65 per cent adjustment of the parties’ net non‑superannuation assets in her favour:[17]
...
The Wife, [Ms Poole], remain appointed Trustee for the sale of the real property known as and situate at [B Street, Suburb C] in the State of Victoria being the whole of the land more particularly described in the Certificate of Title Volume [...] Folio [...] NOTING THAT the same was ordered in paragraph 4 of the Interim Orders made on 21 November 2024.
The Wife, [Ms Poole], be appointed Trustee for the sale of the real property known as and situate at [D Street, Suburb C] in the State of Victoria being the whole of the land more particularly described in the Certificate of Title Volume [...] Folio [...].

...
Any interest which the Husband may have in the real property situate at and known as [E Street, Suburb F] in the State of Victoria (“the [E Street] property”) shall vest absolutely in the Wife.
Subject to order 17 the net proceeds of the sale of the real property situate at and known as [G Street, Suburb C] (“[G Street]”), [D Street] and [B Street] be forthwith paid to the Wife.
Within 7 days of the date of completion of each of the sales of [the D Street property] and [the B Street property], the Husband shall provide the Wife’s solicitors with an accounting from a suitably qualified accountant to advise of the capital gains tax payable with respect to those properties, NOTING it is to be offset against an extant capital loss the husband has AND the Wife’s solicitors shall hold that amount in trust to be paid to the Australian Taxation Office on behalf of the Husband.

...
Within 60 days of the date of these orders (“the date”) the Husband pay to the Wife such further sum to equate to a division 65% of the net non‑superannuation assets of the parties, in favour of the Wife, plus $18,908,06 comprising of:
The sum of $3,000 as to the Wife’s costs reserved on 27 June 2023;
Any costs arising from orders of the Court where costs were reserved by the Court or emanating from the final hearing of these proceedings.
The sum of $3,977.24 in respect to the reserved costs orders of:
i. 9 August 2022

ii. 12.08.2022;

iii. 10 October 2022; and

iv. 29 May 2024

being a total of $15,908.96
(“the payment”)
...
For the purposes of determining the payment to the Wife, the net assets of the parties are as set out in Annexure “A” herein

...
That unless otherwise specified in these orders and save for the purposes of enforcing any moneys due under these or any subsequent orders:
Each party be solely entitled to the exclusion of the other to all superannuation entitlements and other property, including choses-in-action, in the possession of such party as at the date of these orders, the chattels in the:
[E Street] property being deemed to be in the possession of the Wife;
All properties in which the husband holds legal title being deemed to be in the possession of the Husband;
Moneys outstanding to the credit of the parties in any joint bank account are to become the property of the Husband;
insurance policies remain sole property of the owner named thereon;
Each party be solely liable for an indemnify the other against any liability encumbering any item of property to which that party is entitled pursuant to these orders.

On 3 July 2025, the wife filed a Third Further Amended Initiating Application seeking substantially similar orders.
The husband, by way of his Outline of Case filed 27 January 2025, seeks an adjustment of 65 per cent in his favour. He states that the Court should uphold the terms of the financial agreement and that any property settlement should respect the exclusions and conditions outlined in the financial agreement ensuring that the interests of third parties are properly considered. By way of his Amended Response filed 6 June 2024, the husband similarly seeks:
That the Binding Financial Agreement between [MR BRAY] and [MS POOLE] dated 26 August 2010 (the Binding Financial Agreement) be declared as valid and enforceable.
The proceeding is dismissed insofar as it relates to any property that is subject to the Binding Financial Agreement.
There be a just and equitable distribution of the property that is not subject to the binding financial agreement to the parties in a 65/35 division in favour of the father.
The Applicant pay the Respondent compensation pursuant to the personal undertaking provided on 9 August 2022 for the restraints imposed on the property that is subject to the orders made on 9 August 2022 (and as continued thereon).
The Applicant pay the Respondent’s costs on a full indemnity basis.
Such further and other orders as this Honourable Court deems fit.
During closing submissions, counsel for the husband contended that the adjustment the Court should find is largely dependent on the findings the Court makes. It was said that, again, dependent on the findings, an adjustment be made 60 per cent in the husband’s favour.

EVIDENCE

By way of her Outline of Case filed 24 January 2025, the wife relies upon:
(a) Second Further Amended Application filed 30 December 2024;

(b) her affidavit filed 30 December 2024;

(c) her Financial Statement filed 30 December 2024;

(d) Affidavit of Mr EE filed 30 December 2024;

(e) Family Reports by Dr FF dated 2 February 2023 and 2 December 2024; and

(f) Notice of Risk filed 30 November 2022.

By way of his Outline of Case filed 27 January 2025, the husband relies on:
(a) his Financial Statement filed 24 January 2025;

(b) his supplementary affidavit filed 22 January 2025;

(c) his affidavit filed 16 January 2025;

(d) affidavit of Ms GG exhibiting reports regarding supervised visits filed 8 November 2024;

(e) Family Reports by Dr FF dated 2 February 2023 and 2 December 2024;

(f) report by Ms HH regarding supervised visits dated 30 October 2024; and

(g) eight valuation reports by S Comapny.

I refer r 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) which provides that annexures are not in evidence unless they have been tendered. In his affidavit, the husband makes numerous references by way of footnotes to a “tender book”. Notwithstanding, his counsel only tendered three documents. I do not propose, by reason of the Rules, to have regard to this material if it was not tendered during the final hearing.
I note further that the husband produced a very large volume of previously undisclosed documents virtually on the eve of the final hearing to the disadvantage of the wife.

RELEVANT LAW

In respect to the parties' dispute regarding the division of their property in these proceedings, s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) sets out the following:
(1) In property settlement proceedings, the court may make such order as it considers appropriate:
(a) in the case of proceedings with respect to the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them--altering the interests of the parties to the marriage in the property; or
...

including:
(c) an order for a settlement of property in substitution for any interest in the property; and

(d) an order requiring:

(i) either or both of the parties to the marriage; or

...

to make, for the benefit of either or both of the parties to the marriage or a child of the marriage, such settlement or transfer of property as the court determines.

...

(2) The court shall not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.
In exercising that discretion, the Court is required to take into account the matters set out in s 79(4) of the Act, as follows:
(4) In considering what order (if any) should be made under this section in property settlement proceedings, the court shall take into account:
(a) the financial contribution made directly or indirectly by or on behalf of a party to the marriage or a child of the marriage to the acquisition, conservation or improvement of any of the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them, or otherwise in relation to any of that last-mentioned property, whether or not that last-mentioned property has, since the making of the contribution, ceased to be the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(b) the contribution (other than a financial contribution) made directly or indirectly by or on behalf of a party to the marriage or a child of the marriage to the acquisition, conservation or improvement of any of the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them, or otherwise in relation to any of that last-mentioned property, whether or not that last-mentioned property has, since the making of the contribution, ceased to be the property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(c) the contribution made by a party to the marriage to the welfare of the family constituted by the parties to the marriage and any children of the marriage, including any contribution made in the capacity of homemaker or parent; and

(d) the effect of any proposed order upon the earning capacity of either party to the marriage; and

(e) the matters referred to in subsection 75(2) so far as they are relevant; and

(f) any other order made under this Act affecting a party to the marriage or a child of the marriage; and

(g) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the marriage has provided, is to provide, or might be liable to provide in the future, for a child of the marriage.

The High Court in Stanford v Stanford [2012] FamCAFC 1; (2012) 247 CLR 108 at [35] confirmed that before an order is made adjusting the parties' property, the Court is required to make a determination that it is just and equitable to do so. That determination is to be made not as a discrete or preliminary issue, but the Court must consider the matters set out in s 79(4) of the Act.
In Hickey & Hickey & Attorney-General for the Commonwealth of Australia [2003] FamCA 395; (2003) FLC 93-143, the Full Court held at [39] that, in considering the matters set out in section 79(4) of the Act, the preferred approach was to adhere to the following four steps:
(a) identify and determine the value of the asset pool of the parties as at the date of the hearing (this necessarily involves identifying both the assets and liabilities);

(b) identify and assess each of the parties' financial and other contributions up until the date of the hearing (this can include the financial contributions made before, during and after the marriage);

(c) assess how future and other events may have a financial impact on either of the parties, such as their age and state of health and their income and property or financial resources (known as the s 75(2) factors); and

(d) step back and examine this formula-based reasoning against the history of the marriage, intangible considerations and other contingencies so as to consider whether the outcome represents a just and equitable result.

There is a large volume of evidence by each party. I will refer to those matters which are relevant to the determinations that I am required to make and the matters dictated by legislation that I am required to have regard to. A trial judge is not expected to deal with every piece of evidence (Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48); or grapple with every submission (Soulemezis v Dudley (Holdings) Pty Ltd (1987) 10 NSWLR 247).
I will determine the validity of the financial agreement prior to any claim pursuant to s 79 of the Act.

CONSIDERATION OF THE SECTION 90B AGREEMENT

On 26 August 2010, about six weeks prior to the marriage, the parties entered into an agreement titled “SECTION 90B FINANCIAL AGREEMENT PRE-NUPTIAL”. The financial agreement sets out the parties’ assets as follows:
...

[MR BRAY]’s Separate Property

This comprises:
(i) 50% interest of [H Street, Suburb J] in the State of Victoria.

(ii) 100% interest of [1 JJ Street, Suburb Q] in the State of Victoria

(iii) 50% interest of [1 L Street, Suburb N] in the State of Victoria

(iv) 50% interest of [2 L Street, Suburb N] in the state of Victoria

(v) 50% interest of [P Street, Suburb Q] in the state of Victoria

(vi) Any asset which, during the marriage, is received by [MR BRAY] by way of gift or inheritance and remains registered in his name or in the name of an entity ultimately controlled by him.

[MS POOLE]’s Separate Property

This comprises:
(i) Any asset which, during the relationship, is received by [MS POOLE] by way of gift or inheritance and remains registered in her name or in the name of an entity ultimately controlled by her.
Shared property

This includes
(i) [V Street, Suburb Q] in the State of Victoria

(ii) [AA Street, Suburb CC] in the State of Victoria

(iii) [KK Street, Suburb LL] in the state of Victoria

(iv) [MM Street, Suburb NN] in the state of Victoria

(v) Any asset that is not [MR BRAY]’s Separate Property or [Ms Poole]’s Separate Property

(vi) Any asset acquired by the parties or an entity controlled by the parties during the course of the marriage.

...

DIVISION OF ASSETS AND LIABILITIES

6. On Notice, the parties shall divide their Assets as follows:
(i) The parties will each retain their Separate Property.

...
(ii) Shared Property shall be divided equally between the parties. If no agreement is reached as to how any particular item of Shared Property is to be allocated that item will be sold and the proceeds will be divided equally between them.
...
The wife gives evidence that the financial agreement was prepared by Mr K who at the time was a legal professional and had an interest in the H Street property. The Certificate of Independent Legal Advice was signed on her behalf on 21 August 2010 by a solicitor, Mr OO, after she had a walk-in appointment.
The husband asserts that his family expressed concern regarding the impending marriage due to the possible impact on the properties that he held on trust for them.[18] It is his case that they encouraged him to speak with the wife about the possibility of entering into a financial agreement so as to exclude the properties he held on trust for them from the asset pool if they were to separate.[19]
The husband says that he provided the wife with a copy of the draft agreement and they each consulted with their respective families.[20] He gives evidence that he did not want to include the properties at V Street, Suburb Q (“V Street property”) and AA Street, Suburb CC (“AA Street property”) on the list of shared properties but was eventually persuaded by the wife to do so.[21]
He says that he informed the wife that he did not own the trust properties despite being the registered owner and that the wife did not discuss the trust properties with him further as she could see that he was not involved in managing, maintaining, or servicing the mortgages of the properties.[22]
The husband submits that the parties each had independent legal advice prior to entering into the financial agreement and that the wife was aware that Ms O was the equitable owner of the trust properties.[23] He states that the wife also had the benefit of obtaining advice from the maternal grandfather, Mr EE, who is a legal professional in Australia.[24]
The circumstances in which the Court can set aside a financial agreement are set out at s 90K of the Act. This provides:
(1) A court may make an order setting aside a financial agreement or a termination agreement if, and only if, the court is satisfied that:
(a) the agreement was obtained by fraud (including non‑disclosure of a material matter); or

(aa) a party to the agreement entered into the agreement:

(i) for the purpose, or for purposes that included the purpose, of defrauding or defeating a creditor or creditors of the party; or

(ii) with reckless disregard of the interests of a creditor or creditors of the party; or

(ab) a party (the agreement party) to the agreement entered into the agreement:

(i) for the purpose, or for purposes that included the purpose, of defrauding another person who is a party to a de facto relationship with a spouse party; or

(ii) for the purpose, or for purposes that included the purpose, of defeating the interests of that other person in relation to any possible or pending application for an order under section 90SM, or a declaration under section 90SL, in relation to the de facto relationship; or

(iii) with reckless disregard of those interests of that other person; or

(b) the agreement is void, voidable or unenforceable; or

(c) in the circumstances that have arisen since the agreement was made it is impracticable for the agreement or a part of the agreement to be carried out; or

(d) since the making of the agreement, a material change in circumstances has occurred (being circumstances relating to the care, welfare and development of a child of the marriage) and, as a result of the change, the child or, if the applicant has caring responsibility for the child (as defined in subsection (2)), a party to the agreement will suffer hardship if the court does not set the agreement aside; or

(e) in respect of the making of a financial agreement—a party to the agreement engaged in conduct that was, in all the circumstances, unconscionable; or

(f) a payment flag is operating under Part VIIIB on a superannuation interest covered by the agreement and there is no reasonable likelihood that the operation of the flag will be terminated by a flag lifting agreement under that Part; or

(g) the agreement covers at least one superannuation interest that is an unsplittable interest for the purposes of Part VIIIB.

...
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(d), a person has caring responsibility for a child if:
(a) the person is a parent of the child with whom the child lives; or

(b) a parenting order provides that:

(i) the child is to live with the person; or

(ii) the person has parental responsibility for the child.

(3) A court may, on an application by a person who was a party to the financial agreement that has been set aside, or by any other interested person, make such order or orders (including an order for the transfer of property) as it considers just and equitable for the purpose of preserving or adjusting the rights of persons who were parties to that financial agreement and any other interested persons.

...
Section 90KA of the Act relevantly provides that:
The question whether a financial agreement or a termination agreement is valid, enforceable or effective is to be determined by the court according to the principles of law and equity that are applicable in determining the validity, enforceability and effect of contracts and purported contracts, and, in proceedings relating to such an agreement, the court:
(a) subject to paragraph (b), has the same powers, may grant the same remedies and must have the same regard to the rights of third parties as the High Court has, may grant and is required to have in proceedings in connection with contracts or purported contracts, being proceedings in which the High Court has original jurisdiction; and

(b) has power to make an order for the payment, by a party to the agreement to another party to the agreement, of interest on an amount payable under the agreement, from the time when the amount became or becomes due and payable, at a rate not exceeding the rate prescribed by the applicable Rules of Court; and

(c) in addition to, or instead of, making an order or orders under paragraph (a) or (b), may order that the agreement, or a specified part of the agreement, be enforced as if it were an order of the court.

Wife’s Case

The wife submits that the financial agreement should be set aside as:
(a) there is no provision for what should occur if the parties have children. The parties did contemplate having children but now have four with two having extremely high needs. This is in circumstances where the parenting orders now provide for the wife to be the primary carer of the children and that she will suffer hardship pursuant to s 90K(1)(d) if the financial agreement is upheld;

(b) the husband engaged in unconscionable conduct (s 90K(1)(e) of the Act) and/or because of his behaviour by threatening to call off the parties’ wedding unless the wife signed the financial agreement; and

(c) the financial agreement contains multiple untruths, non-disclosure, omissions and errors to render it voidable (s 90K(1)(b) of the Act).

Husband’s Case

The husband contends that:
(a) the financial agreement clearly determines the parties’ entitlements to the asset pool in the event of the breakdown of their marriage;

(b) the financial agreement bars the parties from making any application for a property settlement in respect of their asset pool, restricting the scope of any property claims following separation;

(c) the parties agreed to exclude certain property from the asset pool which were held on trust by the husband for his siblings; and

(d) at the time of the marriage, the wife’s only asset was her superannuation in the sum of $20,000 which the parties agreed to exclude from the asset pool in the financial agreement.

The husband submits that, in light of the above, the financial agreement is legally binding and governs the division of the family assets and excludes any further claims for property settlement and therefore it should be upheld. He opposes the assertions the wife’s above assertions.

Section 90K(1)(d) – Hardship

The wife’s first contention is that the financial agreement made no provision for children where the parties now have four children, two of those having high needs, and where the final parenting orders provide for the wife to have sole parental responsibility and for them to spend limited time with the husband. In these circumstances, she states that if the financial agreement was not aside, she would suffer hardship. This subsection requires that there has been a material change of circumstances and for the hardship to arise “as a result of the change”.
In Fewster & Drake [2016] FamCAFC 214; (2016) FLC 93-745(‘Fewster’), the Full Court held:
The birth of a child leads inexorably to his or her care, development and welfare. We do not see why a birth cannot be a material change in circumstances for the purpose of s 90K(1)(d). Whether it in fact is such a change will depend on all of the circumstances.
...
The husband correctly submits that the words “as a result of the change” indicate that the relevant hardship with which the section is concerned is the hardship which is caused by the change in circumstances. It is the changed circumstances which must give rise to the hardship, and not the agreement itself. It is to be recalled that, subject to compliance with the statutory requirements, people are free to enter such binding financial agreements as they see fit. There is no statutory provision which enables a binding financial agreement to be set aside merely because it is unfair: Hoult & Hoult [2013] FamCAFC 109; (2013) FLC 93-546 at 87,283 and 87,296 - 87,298.

...
We turn now to the second aspect of this challenge. The concluding words of s 90K(1)(d) are “if the court does not set the agreement aside”. Logically and inevitably those words require the court to undertake some comparison between the position of the child, or the person with caring responsibility, if the agreement remains in place and the position of that child or person if the agreement is set aside. It is only by doing so that the court can place itself in a position to determine whether there will be hardship if the agreement is not set aside. The primary judge did not undertake such a comparison.
Finally, we accept the husband’s submission that the hardship required by the section is something more than unfairness. In In the Marriage of Whitford [1979] FamCA 3; (1979) FLC 90-612 (“Whitford”) at 78,144-78,145 the Court said that hardship is:
...akin to such concepts as hardness, severity, privation, that which is hard to bear or a substantial detriment...

...

In ordinary parlance, hardship means something more burdensome than “any appreciable detriment'”. We consider that in subsec. 44(4) the word should have its usual, though not necessarily its most stringent, connotations.
Although Whitford was a case dealing with s 44(4) and applications for leave to institute property proceedings, these passages are relevant to s 90K(1)(d), as they discuss ‘hardship’ in the context of its ordinary meaning. There is nothing in the terms of s 90K that suggests a different approach should be taken.
At the time of the financial agreement, there were no children to the marriage. The financial agreement states that the parties have no children and does not make reference to any intention of the parties to have children. The husband claims that he was initially opposed to including the V Street and AA Street properties as shared properties in the financial agreement but acceded to the wife’s request to do so after agreeing that once married she would make significant sacrifices in terms of her career and physical needs to have and raise children.[25] Even if I were to accept, which the wife does not cavil with, that the parties intended to have children at the time of the financial agreement and this was indirectly accounted for, it could not have been reasonably contemplated by the parties that two of the children would have significantly high needs nor that the wife would largely have the care of the children. The children’s diagnoses are set out at [5]. I find that the birth of the parties’ four children, with two having high needs, and the wife having primary care of the children with limited support from the husband is a material change of circumstances.
Turning to the question of whether as a result of this change the wife will suffer hardship if the financial agreement is not set aside, the wife gives evidence that she is solely responsible for all the children’s educational expenses to the sum of $65,000 per annum.[26] In relation to each of the children she states:[27]
(a) following separation W was seeing a counsellor on a weekly basis. He then started seeing a psychologist and now sees the school counsellor. He suffers from eczema which requires regular management and an allergy;

(b) as noted above, X has been diagnosed with ASD. The wife says that he is highly intelligent although rigid in his beliefs and behaviour and thrives on structure. He is progressing well academically but receives extra support for reading comprehension at school. He attends, funded by the NDIS, a psychologist, a speech therapist, a therapy assistant, a speech pathologist and the school counsellor;

(c) as noted above, Y has been diagnosed with ASD and ADHD. The wife says that he has significant challenges academically and has a severe learning disability. He attends, funded by the NDIS, a behavioural therapist, a speech therapist, a therapy assistant, a speech pathologist and has an integration aide at school. He has also been diagnosed with epilepsy; and

(d) following separation, Z has been seeing a counsellor and is now being supported by a psychologist. The wife says that he is progressing well academically.

By way of her Financial Statement filed on 31 December 2024, the wife deposes to an average weekly income of $2,596 and weekly personal expenditure of $3,867. Her weekly income includes the benefits received for X and Y. She states that as she is meeting the entirety of the children’s school fees and has had to draw down on the mortgage registered over the E Street property.[28] By way of his Financial Statement filed 24 January 2025, the husband lists his weekly income as $908 and his personal expenditure as $3,969. He states that he pays $150 weekly for all the children. His child support payments are currently assessed at $177 per month with the husband being $47,428.08 in arrears.[29] In circumstances where the wife gives evidence that her annual income from her salary as an educator is $116,000, the children’s school fees alone amount to over half of her salary. It is clear that her current financial position is not sustainable for her or the children.
In accordance with Fewster at [67], I have regard to the position of the children, or the wife being their primary carer, if the financial agreement were to remain in place or if it were to be set aside. The financial agreement excludes the wife from any claim to “[Mr Bray]’s Separate Property”. The wife exhibits, now marked as A38, a table outlining the currently equity in the assets in the financial agreement with the separate property of the husband totalling $818,720.
Having regard to the above matters, I find that as a result of the material change in circumstances, the wife will suffer hardship if the financial agreement is not set aside.

Section 90K(1)(b) and (e) – Undue Influence And Unconscionable Conduct

Undue influence and unconscionable conduct may arise from the same set of facts (Thorne v Kennedy [2017] HCA 49; (2017) 263 CLR 85 (‘Thorne’)). Undue influence focuses of the state of mind of the party who is in a weaker position whilst unconscionable conduct relates to actions of the stronger party (Commercial Bank of Australia Ltd v Amadio [1983] HCA 14; (1983) 151 CLR 447).

Undue Influence

In Thorne, and in relation to undue influence, the plurality stated:
In 1836, in a passage which was copied verbatim by Snell thirty years later, Story said that a person can be subjected to undue influence where the effect of factors such as pressure is that the person “has no free will, but stands in vinculis [in chains]”. He explained that “the constant rule in Equity is, that, where a party is not a free agent, and is not equal to protecting himself, the Court will protect him”. In 1866, this approach was applied in equity by the House of Lords, recognising undue influence in a case of pressure that deprived the plaintiff of “free agency”. In 1868, in probate, Sir James Wilde also described undue influence as arising where a person is not a “free agent”. In Johnson v Buttress, Dixon J described how undue influence could arise from the “deliberate contrivance” of another (which naturally includes pressure) giving rise to such influence over the mind of the other that the act of the other is not a “free act”. And, in Bank of New South Wales v Rogers, McTiernan J characterised the absence of undue influence as a “free and well-understood act” and Williams J referred to “the free exercise of the respondent’s will”.
The question whether a person’s act is “free” requires consideration of the extent to which the person was constrained in assessing alternatives and deciding between them. Pressure can deprive a person of free choice in this sense where it causes the person substantially to subordinate his or her will to that of the other party. It is not necessary for a conclusion that a person’s free will has been substantially subordinated to find that the party seeking relief was reduced entirely to an automaton or that the person became a “mere channel through which the will of the defendant operated”. Questions of degree are involved. But, at the very least, the judgmental capacity of the party seeking relief must be “markedly sub-standard” as a result of the effect upon the person’s mind of the will of another.
...
...For example, in undue influence there will be questions of evaluative judgment involved in assessing whether the extent to which a person’s will has been subordinated to another’s is sufficient to characterise the person as lacking free will...

(footnotes omitted)
The wife’s claim that the husband threatened to call the wedding off unless she signed the financial agreement.
The wife says that when she visited the husband whilst he was living with Ms O in Suburb C, the husband raised at about midnight the topic of the financial agreement whereby the wife says she informed the husband that she did not want to sign the financial agreement. The wife states that the husband responded in a stern manner that if she did not sign the financial agreement the wedding would be cancelled. She states that she left at about 1:00am feeling uneasy and worried where:[30]
(a) invitations to 450 guests had already been sent;

(b) five limousines for the wedding party had been booked;

(c) the location for the civil wedding ceremony had been booked; and

(d) a Country PP Ceremony had already occurred signifying a spiritual union between the parties.

The wife gives evidence that she felt that she had no other option but to sign the financial agreement as if the wedding had been called off as threatened by the husband she would have lost “considerable face” in the Country PP community which she was not prepared to entertain.[31]
I do not find that evidence establishes that the wife was subject to undue influence. Whilst the wife would have undoubtedly be wanting to marry the husband, I do not find that that the wife has established that losing “face” within her community and possible cancellation of the wedding, considering all the other surrounding factors, to be such to give rise to undue influence.

Unconscionable Conduct

In Thorne, the plurality outlined in relation to unconscionable conduct:
A conclusion of unconscionable conduct requires the innocent party to be subject to a special disadvantage “which seriously affects the ability of the innocent party to make a judgment as to [the innocent party’s] own best interests”. The other party must also unconscientiously take advantage of that special disadvantage. This has been variously described as requiring “victimisation”, “unconscientious conduct”, or “exploitation”. Before there can be a finding of unconscientious taking of advantage, it is also generally necessary that the other party knew or ought to have known of the existence and effect of the special disadvantage.
Counsel for the husband submits that there is no basis for any assertion that the wife was under a special disadvantage. Rather, counsel for the husband states that the wife was merely concerned about embarrassment regarding the cancellation of the wedding. In relation to the legal advice obtained by the wife, counsel for the husband contends that the wife did not complain about the quality of the advice that she received. When it was raised with counsel for the husband that the advice could have been given on materially incorrect information where it did not identify third party interests in certain properties, it was asserted that it is irrelevant where there is no causation as the wife regardless of any finding that is made. The wife contended that the husband took advantage of her to obtain the financial agreement which was wholly inadequate and involved gross inequality.
Although the document was presented to the wife, very shortly prior to the planned wedding, and there were statements made that the wedding would not proceed if the wife did not agree, I do not find that the wife was subject to a special disadvantage as that expression is used in the authorities.

Section 90K(1)(b) – The Agreement is Void, Voidable or Unenforceable

The wife contends that by reason of the terms of the financial agreement, it ought to be set aside pursuant to s 90K(1)(b) of the Act. Section 90KA of the Act relevantly provides:
The question whether a financial agreement or a termination agreement is valid, enforceable or effective is to be determined by the court according to the principles of law and equity that are applicable in determining the validity, enforceability and effect of contracts and purported contracts...
The wife asserts that the financial agreement contains a multiplicity of untruths, non-disclosure of material matters, omissions and errors such that it is voidable. The wife refers to the following matters in her Outline of Case:
(i) Most importantly, nowhere in the agreement did the husband disclose that the assets he was described as being the “owner of, or otherwise entitled to” were held by him on trust for his sister (which is not conceded). The Wife’s case is that this is not true.

(ii) Husband held himself out to the Wife, and stated in the agreement, that he had never married. That was untrue. The husband has never disclosed the financial circumstances relating to that marriage.

(iii) The “shared property” does not have a value placed upon it.
(iv) The manner in which paragraph 6 deals with “shared property” conflicts with paragraph 8;

(v) The values ascribed to the Husband’s assets is unclear, given he is said to only own a proportion of some of them.

(vi) Properties are listed as shared property, which were not yet the property of the parties albeit subject to purchase contract.

It was submitted by the wife that the errors were such to induce the wife to enter into the financial agreement.
In the financial agreement, the husband asserts ownership in properties where in these proceedings he now claims that he has no interest in these properties. Again, when it was raised with counsel for the husband that, for example, in the financial agreement he asserts a 50 percent interest in the H Street property, yet in these proceedings he claims no interest in the property, counsel for the husband submitted that this is immaterial as the property was included in the husband’s separate property. Counsel for the husband further submitted that this reflected legal title but not did not reflect equitable title.
I find that the significant misstatement of the husband’s true financial position in the document is such that the financial agreement ought to be set aside. The representations contained in the document are not relevant solely to what would occur on separation. They are relevant to the wife assessing the value of the husband’s financial position prior to entering into the financial agreement.
For all these reasons, the financial agreement should be set aside.
