From the above, it is plain that the intention of the order was for the husband to join any third party if he was to assert the claims he now does. The husband’s assertion that the decision to not join any third party was a tactical decision of the wife is without foundation.
Further, when it was raised that the term of the orders of the SJR placed the obligation on the husband, counsel for the husband submitted that this is irrelevant where, first, the orders were interlocutory and cannot bind a trial judge and, second, it does not deflect from the Court’s statutory obligation to determine the existing legal and equitable interests of the parties’ property. Otherwise, the husband submits that whilst Ms O did not give evidence, that:
(a) there has been no contrary evidence and reliance can be placed on the husband’s evidence;

(b) there is no need to join any third party as you do not have to continually prove something and it has already been proven by the husband’s evidence and a document; and

(c) there can also be explanations for the rule in Jones v Dunkel [1959] HCA 8; (1959) 101 CLR 298 (“Jones v Dunkel”) and why a person would not be called to give evidence.

The husband produced a trust deed between him and Ms O executed on 12 September 2007 which records that a 50 percent interest in the H Street property was transferred to the husband to hold on trust for Ms O and that she provided the purchase money and all other associated costs.[54] The document referred to was produced very shortly prior to the commencement of the final hearing.
The husband made a decision to either not join or call Ms O or other parties to support his assertions. The husband sought to rely on Masoud & Masoud [2016] FamCAFC 24; (2016) FLC 93-689 (‘Masoud’) at [51], to support the proposition that there was no need to call them as the husband had given evidence of the relevant arrangements. In Masoud, the issue concerned a proof of a loan between a party and their parents. The loan was recorded in writing (and it was not suggested that the loan was a sham) and one of the parents gave evidence about the provision of funds. Subsequently, a ground of appeal was that as both parents had not given evidence, a Jones v Dunkel inference should have been made. The Full Court held at [53] that no inference could be drawn. In the present case, the only person who gave evidence is the husband. There is a contest about the authenticity of the loan agreements and arrangements generally. The husband, prior to the marriage, prepared the financial agreement the details of which, regarding the property he asserted that he owned at that time, directly contradicts the evidence that he now gives about the ownership of properties. I accept that a Jones v Dunkel inference ought to be drawn. The husband could not offer an explanation for why Ms O was not called. I also find there to be merit in the submission that the husband would not have wanted to call/join Ms O to the proceedings where, for example, there is evidence of him holding himself out to be the landlord of a property which he asserts that Ms O owns.[55] I will include these items in the balance sheet.
The husband’s assertion that the orders requiring him to join any party in the event that he wished to prove a trust were not binding on him as:
(a) they were made by a senior judicial registrar;

(b) they are interlocutory;

(c) interlocutory orders cannot bind a trial judge with the submission being made that to suggest they do so was a "novel proposition";[56] and

(d) the orders cannot deflect from the Court’s statutory obligation to identify the existing legal and equitable interests of the parties.

are similarly without foundation.

An extant order is binding on parties unless set aside and that is the case whether orders are made by a registrar or another judge. Orders for the joinder of third parties will always be, by their nature, interlocutory. The husband was required to join parties affected in the event that he was to assert that properties held in his name were held on their behalf. The Court in making those orders was seeking to efficiently manage the litigation and to compel the husband to join the parties necessary to prove his claim and inferentially those parties who may be affected by orders.
Each of the parties who are said to be affected by the trust/the co-owners are close relatives of the husband some of whom filed affidavits in the interlocutory phases of this proceeding.
Counsel for the husband made submissions regarding the effect of Chickabo Pty Ltd v Zphere Pty Ltd (No 2) [2019] VSC 580 at [41], [44]-[53], [101]-[103], [107]-[109] and [112]-[115] (“Chickabo”). He asserted that the Court could not make orders in the absence of the third parties which may affect them. It was also asserted any finding could not bind third parties and they would be entitled as of right to set aside any findings. An invitation was extended to have the proceeding adjourned to allow those parties to be joined and that was rejected on the basis that there would be an apprehension of bias given that I had heard most of the evidence and it was asserted that the affected third parties would need a “full new trial. They need to do a tracing exercise. They need to go through – do chapter and verse. The matter would need to be adjourned for a year or two.”[57]
Chickabo is a decision at first instance of Sifris J. That case concerned plaintiffs who were partners in an accounting firm. They alleged that another partner and entities associated with him obtained unauthorised profits exceeding $11,000,000 in breach of fiduciary duties. Reasons were delivered and findings were made of breach of fiduciary duty although no final orders were made against the defendant partner. The plaintiffs in the case then joined the defendant’s wife and adult children and claims were made against them as volunteer recipients. The claim was made against those parties alleging that they had received traceable proceeds of the unauthorised profits initially obtained by the defendant.
The joined parties made an application to Sifris J for an order that his Honour’s judgment be set aside and/or an order that there be a new trial on all issues before a different judge. The joined parties contended that they were necessary parties from the outset of the proceedings and accordingly submitted that there had to be new a new trial against all defendants .In the alternative, it was claimed that the joined parties were not bound by the findings made by Sifris J and for that reason they should either be removed from the proceedings or cease being parties to the proceedings.
Justice Sifris dismissed the joined parties’ application and held that the joined parties were not necessary parties to the proceeding as originally formulated and that they were not directly affected either by the relief sought in the proceeding before they were joined or by the findings made against the defendant. The only party directly affected by the plaintiff's claims was the defendant.
The effect of the decision of Sifris J at first instance was directly contrary to the submissions that were made by counsel for the husband in relation to that case. What counsel for the husband also failed to mention was that the matter was appealed and Sifris J’s decision was set aside: FBR Fund Administration Pty Ltd v Chickabo Pty Ltd [2019] VSCA 314.
In my view, both the decision at first instance and the decision of the Court of Appeal are not directly relevant to the exercise the Court is required to embark on in the present case. The husband has not proved the trust which he asserts because he has failed to call evidence to prove that claim. He who asserts must prove.
In circumstances where the husband has not joined parties or where those parties have not sought to intervene is akin to established principles in probate proceedings as noted in Boyd v Thorn [2017] NSWCA 210; (2017) 96 NSWLR 390 (‘Boyd v Thorn’) at [95] per Leeming J where he referred to Osborne v Smith [1960] HCA 89; (1960) 105 CLR 153 as authority for the proposition:
there is a well-established principle of probate practice, which grew up in the ecclesiastical courts, that any person having an interest may have himself made a party by intervening, and that if he, knowing what was passing, does not intervene, but is "content to stand by and let his battle be fought by somebody else in the same interest", he is bound by the result, and is not to be allowed to re-open the case.
That principle has been extended beyond probate cases: Nana Ofori Atta II v Nana Abu Bonsra II [1958] AC 95 (PC) at 102; Administration of the Territory of Papua New Guinea v Daera Guba (1973) 130 CLR 353 at 403 and 456; House of Spring Gardens Ltd v Waite [1991] 1 QB 241 (CA) at 253-4; Ann Street Mezzanine Pty Ltd v Beck [2009] FCA 333; (2009) 175 FCR 532 at [31]- [34] (Finkelstein J.)
In the course of final submissions, the husband by his counsel rejected the invitation to join the members of the husband’s family who were said to be affected by the orders proposed by the wife. The final hearing was adjourned to allow the intervention of the ATO and no application was made by any of the members of the husband’s family to intervene at that stage nor did the husband make an application to join them.
Because of the positive claims being made by the husband in relation to the trusts and loans from related parties, the husband was in effect the plaintiff in respect of those claims. As is noted by Leeming J in Boyd v Thorn at [94]: it is for the plaintiff to join all necessary parties: Merit Protection Commissioner v Nonnenmacher [1999] FCA 274; (1999) 86 FCR 112 at 117; Ross v Lane Cove Council [2014] NSWCA 50; (2014) 86 NSWLR 34 at [51].
I do not accept the husband’s contentions in relation to these properties and they ought to be included in the balance sheet. The orders proposed by the wife, contemplates that if the wife seeks to sell a jointly owned property, she will institute proceedings against the co-owner of the property in order to do so, presumably by way of partition proceedings pursuant to s 225 of the Property Law Act 1958 (Vic). This will give those parties an opportunity to he heard in respect of that application.

Item 15

Item 15 of the balance sheet relates to a series of loans the husband claims were advanced to him by family members in connection with the D Street property. The evidence adduced by the husband in support of these alleged loans is lacking in detail. The husband’s evidence is that in October 2014 his sister, Ms BB, purchased the property for $1,450,000, paying a deposit of $70,000, on the basis that she anticipated a profit following renovation and resale. He further states that in early 2015, at a time when he was experiencing financial difficulties and with the wife’s consent, he approached Ms BB to discuss the possibility of participating in her investment in the D Street property. According to the husband, Ms BB agreed to include both him and Ms O in the project.
The husband gives evidence that Ms O ultimately contributed $290,292 towards the purchase price and that neither he nor the wife contributed funds towards the acquisition of the property, aside from about $150,000 said to have been expended on renovations, which he describes as representing the entirety of his financial contribution.
The husband states that the property was initially rented as a rooming house and that thereafter his sisters attended the property up to three times per week to oversee renovation works, including providing refreshments to tradespeople. He says that in late 2021 he and his sisters concluded that the property was operating at a significant loss and that the most appropriate course was to invest approximately $50,000 in further renovations and then sell the property. He states that renovation works commenced in April 2022, with an auction originally anticipated for July 2022.
The alleged liability is therefore said to comprise:
(a) $290,292 representing Ms O’s contribution to the purchase;

(b) $70,000 representing Ms BB’s deposit;

(c) $55,276 representing Ms BB’s mortgage repayments; and

(d) $66,460 representing Ms O’s mortgage repayments.

The wife disputes the inclusion of this alleged liability on the basis that there is insufficient evidence to substantiate the husband’s claims and that neither Ms O nor Ms BB has been called to give evidence. She disputes that she was provided with evidence of the detail of any of these transactions (which evidence I accept).
The D Street property is registered in the husband’s sole name.
The Court has a discretion to ignore, or to discount, an unsecured liability where it is appropriate to do so in the circumstances: Biltoft & Biltoft [1995] FamCAFC 45; (1995) FLC 92-614. Further, an unsecured debt may be allocated exclusively to one party and met from that party’s share of the property pool: Puddy & Grossvard and Anor [2010] FamCAFC 54; (2010) FLC 93-432 at [62] and [101]–[111]; Trustee for the Bankrupt Estate of Lasic v Lasic [2009] FamCAFC 64; (2009) FLC 93-402 at [198]–[200]. Circumstances in which a court may disregard an unsecured liability include:
(a) where enforcement is unlikely, such as in the case of loans from parents to a child, particularly where the parents are financially secure and repayment is contemplated only at the child’s convenience;

(b) where the liability arises from a deliberate or reckless disregard of the other party’s potential entitlement under ss 79 or 90SM;

(c) where the liability has been unreasonably incurred;

(d) where the liability bears no connection to the relationship;

(e) where the liability equals or nearly equals the value of the property otherwise available for division, particularly where this would prejudice a party who was unaware of the liability or its extent; and

(f) where the liability is vague, uncertain or insufficiently particularised.

The husband has not called either Ms O or Ms BB to give evidence to establish the existence of the alleged loans. There is no written documentation evidencing any agreement in relation to the alleged investments. In the absence of proof, I am not satisfied that the alleged liability has been established and it will therefore be disregarded. None of the purported creditors have produced evidence of any demand for repayment or otherwise participated in the proceedings.

ITEMS 21, 22, 23, 24

These items are debts arising from the use of credit cards by the husband and the wife. It is the wife’s assertion that as these arose post separation, she seeks that they be excluded as there is no evidence as to what the parties spent the monies on.
The husband has not given evidence of how the money obtained through credit cards was spent. I will not include these amounts in the asset pool as liabilities.

ITEM 25

This relates to an alleged loan the husband received from the paternal grandparents. He seeks that it be included in the asset pool in the sum of $327,000.
The husband gives evidence that throughout the marriage he borrowed money from his parents, including for wedding expense, purchase of properties, holidays and for vehicles and that these constitute the sum referred to above.[58] These sums have not been repaid by the husband.
The wife submits that there is a lack of evidence to support the husband’s claim. She raises the presumption of advancement and also there having been no attempt by the husband to repay these amounts and that they are likely statute barred as unsecured loans.
It is noted, on the husband’s evidence, that these alleged loans range from 2009-2016.
Given the length of time since the funds were allegedly advanced and the lack of evidence of any demand for repayment or a requirement that interest be paid, I find that these loans ought to be disregarded as a liability (see: Hanigan & Hanigan (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1F 516).

ITEM 26

Item 26 is a loan that the husband asserts he received from Ms O in sum of $471,729. The husband has tendered a loan agreement dated 8 March 2016,[59] the terms of which are for Ms O to provide the husband with $762,021.12 for a loan term of eight years with 6 percent interest annually. The loan is purported to be secured (on the husband’s submission by operation of clause 2.4) over B Street, Suburb C (“B Street property”) and the D Street, G Street and E Street properties.
The loan agreement sets out that the amount of $762,021.12 had been paid to the husband as follows:
(a) Ms O had withdrawn $290,292 from her bank account to pay for the balance of the D Street property settlement on 30 June 2015; and

(b) Ms O had received the sum of $514,000 from a refinance of a property at 2 JJ Street, Suburb Q on 16 February 2016 and transferred this sum to the husband in three payments on 16 February and 8 March 2016 for the settlement on the G Street and E Street properties.

Clause 2.4 provides:
2.4 The Borrower hereby agrees use the Security Properties including the [B Street, D Street, G Street] and the [E Street] Property as the charge of the loan and agrees to the Lender loading the registerable Caveats or other charges against the said interest of the Security Property to further and better secure the rights of the Lender.
The husband has produced documents showing a loan drawdown from Ms O’s bank account on 16 February 2016 in the sum of $514,000[60] as well as the withdrawal of $290,292 on 29 June 2015.[61]
The wife responds to these claims as she did to item 25, including, noting that there is no evidence from Ms O, no evidence of any repayment and that the loan is statute barred. She also rejects that the loan is secured, noting that whilst the loan makes provision for the securing of the loan, that no steps were taken to do such and that there is no intention or capacity to enforce the loan.
No caveat or other charge has been lodged against what was described as the secured property.
The husband was put on notice that the wife regarded the document as a sham. The documents were produced for the first time shortly prior to final hearing in circumstances where it had not been previously referred to. There is no evidence that interest was paid notwithstanding the agreement provides that interest would be charged at the rate of 6 percent on the loan amount. The husband asks the Court to take into account the liability in the amount of $762,021.12 without interest being alleged to be owing on that sum.
In circumstances where the husband was on notice as a result of the evidence of the wife that the existence of the loan was not accepted, he could have called Ms O to give evidence regarding the circumstances of entering into the written agreement to formally prove the document. Whilst a document may be proved pursuant to s 48 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), that section does not address any need to satisfy the Court of the authenticity of the document: National Australia Bank v Rusu [1999] NSWSC 539; (1999) 47 NSWLR 309. I do not accept that the production of the loan agreement establishes that the loan agreement was entered into or that the sums referred to in it are a secured or unsecured debt. The husband made a calculated decision not to call Ms O and by not calling her he has not proved their alleged loan. I will disregard this alleged loan as a liability as the existence of the loan has not been proved.

ITEM 27

Item 27, being $545,920 arising from a tax liability, became a significant issue in the proceedings. In late 2024 the husband was served with a writ issued by the Australia Tax Office (“ATO”) filed in the County Court of Victoria claiming a debt of $183,107.04.[62] He says that this arose out of income tax liabilities for the financial years ending in 2015, 2016 and 2017, however, that he has now also completed tax returns for the financial years ending in 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023 and 2024 with his current tax liability including accrued General Interest Charge as at 30 June 2025 being $574,963.38.[63]
There was significant dispute as to how this liability should be treated, and, on 17 February 2025, judgment was reserved on the proviso that the husband was to provide the ATO notification of these proceedings, a copy of the balance sheet and the orders sought by both parties. The Commissioner of Taxation (“the Commissioner”) was then given 28 days from the date of service to seek to intervene in these proceedings.
The Commissioner filed a Notice of Intervention on 19 March 2025. On 21 March 2025, orders were made granting the Commissioner leave to intervene in the proceedings. The Commissioner filed an affidavit on 14 May 2025 where it was noted that the amount of $566,407.24 is owing.
The matter was listed for mention on 12 June 2025. On that date, orders were made for the Commissioner and the parties to file and serve submissions as to orders sought. The Commissioner seeks that the debt owing be paid from the proceeds of sale of the G Street and B Street properties.
The wife submits:
(a) she has no issues with the nature and quantum of the tax liabilities; and

(b) she contends that the payment should be made from the sale of the D Street property and limited to the sum of $276,808.65.

She asserts that the Commissioner’s interests have no priority over her claim pursuant to s 79 of the Act and that the liability, minus the general interest of charges and penalties and taking account of tax paid or credits, is $276,808.65, and that it is this amount which should be paid in priority to the wife. Further, due to the conduct of the husband, she contends that the debt should be paid from the husband’s share of the property
The husband submits:
(a) the Court should make an order that the liability be paid to the Commissioner, however, that the Commissioner should not be placed in a preferred position to any of the creditors by having their debt secured against the net proceeds of sale of the G Street and B Street properties; and

(b) that the liability should be paid from the parties’ assets.

The husband is also seeking an order that County Court proceedings commenced by the Commissioner be subject to an anti-suit injunction.
The Commissioner submits:
(a) since the affidavit of 14 May 2025, $574,963.38 remains owing up to and including 30 June 2025;

(b) the Commissioner rejects the wife’s contention that the amount should be limited to $276,808.65 and asserts that it is appropriate that the liability be paid in full from the parties’ asset pool; and

(c) the Commissioner agrees with the wife’s submissions that:

(i) the Court may distinguish between the primary tax liabilities and penalty amounts;

(ii) the Court may treat certain liabilities as a liability of one party and not the other, where there are good reasons to do so; and

(iii) third party creditors and party spouses do not enjoy any right of priority over the other in proceedings under s 79 of the Act.

(d) the Commissioner opposes an anti-suit injunction as sought by the husband where the proceedings in the County Court concern a different matter.

The Commissioner submits, however, that the wife’s assertion that an order for payment for the entirety of the tax liability would lead to a result which is not just and equitable is illogical when she subsequently submits that the payment of the liability be met entirely from the husband’s share of the property pool. It is further submitted that this is particularly so where the wife seeks a 65 per cent adjustment in her favour of the property pool as asserted by her which would provide her with about $2,500,000 and that this nullifies any concerns raised about priority.
The Commissioner is a creditor of the husband. I do not accept the wife’s calculations based on figures out in affidavit of Ms SS affirmed 14 May 2025, that the income tax payable is $276,808.65. The income tax payable is $301,918.90 but the wife has subtracted from that sum, payments/credits in the sum of $25,110.25 from that figure to reach $276,808.65. I find it appropriate that the sum of $301,918.90 be deducted from the sale of the D Street property.
The evidence does not support a finding that the husband kept the wife informed about the extent of the taxation liabilities that were accruing or the penalties that were being imposed as a result of his failure in attending to pay taxes or alternatively that he brought the taxation liabilities sufficiently to the attention of the wife so that arrangements could be made to deal with the mounting liabilities. I was referred to no evidence by the husband which would support a finding that he had kept the wife informed of such matters and there is certainly no documentary evidence of that tendered by the husband. In those circumstances the husband should assume sole liability for the outstanding amount being in the sum of $273,044.48 and that should be excluded from the balance sheet. The income tax component ought to be deducted from the proceeds of sale of the D Street property and paid to the ATO.

ITEMS 29 AND 30

These items relate to land tax liabilities (separate from the liability at item 28) that the husband says he owes as his portion with Mr K and Ms O which totals $64,739. The wife submits these only appeared in the husband’s material shortly prior to the hearing. She submits that these ought to be excluded where it is clear that the husband, Mr K and Ms O have consciously decided not to pay land tax whilst these proceedings are underway. This is an arrangement between the husband, Mr K and Ms O and I will exclude this liability from the balance sheet.

ITEMS 31 AND 32

These items relate to loans the husband says he received from Mr K and Ms BB in the sum of $140,000 and $86,000 respectively. The wife submits that Mr K and Ms BB did not give evidence in relation to these loans and there is insufficient evidence of same.
It appears that these were loans alleged to be provided to the husband by Ms BB and Mr K for the purchase of the B Street property.[64] Again, there is a lack of evidence from Ms BB and Mr K in relation to these alleged loans and accordingly the husband has failed to prove them and they will be disregarded.

ITEMS 9, 10, 11, 11A

These items relate to various addbacks sought by each of the parties.

Add backs

The final hearing in this matter commenced prior to the legislative changes on 10 June 2025.
The wife seeks add backs for:
(a) the 3 L Street property in the sum of $460,028;

(b) the V Street property in the sum of $397,000; and

(c) the AA Street property in the sum of $313,494.

The husband seeks an add back for:
(a) home loan redraws which the husband says the wife used for legal fees in the sum of $124,000.
When making an order for property settlement, the court is required to have regard to what is just and equitable in the circumstances of each case. This includes whether to exercise a discretion to notionally add back property which has been dissipated prior to the hearing relying on s 79/90SM(4) or 75(2)(o)/90SF(3)(r) of the Act. While notional property is not available for distribution at the time of the hearing, it may be required to be accounted for, for the purpose of considering what distribution is just and equitable in the relevant circumstances.
The Full Court in Omacini & Omacini [2005] FamCAFC 104; (2005) FLC 93-218 at [30], identified three categories where the court has notionally added back property, being:
(a) legal fees

(b) premature distribution of matrimonial finds

(c) “waste” or wanton, negligent, or reckless dissipation of assets

At [39] the Court cautioned that an amount should not be added back solely because a party has expended money realised from the disposal of an asset that existed at the date of separation. Rather, it is necessary to look at the reasonableness of the distribution of those funds. Indeed, it is widely recognised that add backs are the exception, and not the norm (C & C [1998] FamCA 143 at [46]; Vass & Vass [2015] FamCAFC 51; (2015) 53 Fam LR 373 at [138]; Rankin & Rankin (2017) FLC 93-766 at [57]-[58]). The Court must endeavour to reflect the circumstances of the parties at the time of the trial, recognising that parties do not “go into a state of suspended economic animation” on separation (M & M [1998] FamCA 42 at [2.11]).
In Chorn & Hopkins [2004] FamCA 633; (2004) FLC 93-204 the Full Court at [56] stated it is a matter of discretion for the trial judge whether to notionally add property into the asset pool, and any funds added back must be connected to the marriage/de facto relationship. The Full Court noted in M & M at [2.11] that “reasonably incurred living expenses” will not be added back.
Legal fees are often sought to be added back. The Full Court in DJM v JLM (1998) FLC 92‑816 at [11.6] outlined that there is a policy reason for allowing such add backs under the Act:
... s 117 provides that each party to proceedings under the Family Law Act shall bear their own costs unless the court otherwise orders. Failing to add‐back moneys expended by parties on costs frequently has the effect of defeating the policy of s 117 by permitting the pool of available assets for distribution between the parties to be diminished by any moneys that either of the parties have managed to spend on their costs up to the date of trial. ...
In Stanford & Stanford, The High Court at [37] held that property settlements must deal with the “existing legal and equitable interests of the parties in the property” (emphasis added), which raised concerns about whether there is still a role for add backs.
The more recent decision of the Full Court in Trevi & Trevi [2018] FamCAFC 173 at [30] provided further clarity:
Two fundamental premises emerge from Omacini and the authorities preceding it. First, "adding back" is a discretionary exercise. When the discretion is exercised in favour of adding back, it reflects a decision that, exceptionally, in the particular circumstances of a case, justice and equity requires it. The second premise is its corollary: in cases that are not "exceptional", justice and equity can be achieved, not by adding back, but by the exercise of a different discretion - usually by taking up the same as a relevant section 75(2) factor. Indeed, it has been said that the latter is “a course which is, perhaps, technically more correct” than adding back to the list of existing interests in property.
(Footnotes omitted)

3 L Street

The 3 L Street property was sold in mid-2022 for $450,000[65] to the husband’s nephew due to what he contends to be financial burdens. That sale occurred, on the wife’s evidence, three days after receiving correspondence from the wife’s former solicitors and four days after separation. The property was purchased in early 2017 for $550,000. The wife contends the husband sold it unilaterally for $100,000 less than the purchase price. The husband contends that he and the wife agreed to sell the property to the husband’s nephew in mid-2022 but has produced no evidence of how that agreement was made. I do not accept that that is the case and find that the wife became aware of the sale after the event.
The wife tendered a retrospective valuation produced pursuant to orders made on 6 June 2024 that valued the property as of mid-2022, at $800,000.[66] However, it is the husband’s evidence that the property was in a dilapidated condition. Again, the wife points to the husband not calling the nephew to give evidence. Not only did the husband not call the nephew to give evidence, but he also did not provide any evidence as to the condition of the property as at the time of sale to support his assertion. The valuation was based on a kerbside assessment and marketing material from November 2022. I find that the property was sold by the husband to a relative for less than market value and that the value as of mid-2022 was $800,000 rather than the sale price of $450,000. The sum of $350,000 is effectively wasted by the conduct of the husband and ought to be added back in the balance sheet.

V Street property

As to the V Street property, the husband sold this property to his other sister, Ms TT, in 2019. It was subsequently valued at $700,000, but it was sold for less as the husband said he had agreed to sell it to Ms TT in 2014 for $400,000 but delayed transferring into her name due to the costs of breaking the mortgage early being about $60,000. During that time, Ms TT paid the mortgage and outgoings and received all the rent. In late 2019, he transferred the property into Ms TT’s name, and she paid out the mortgage of about $303,000 and the husband asserts that she provided the balance to him by way of $100,000 in cash. The wife submits that if the husband wanted to sell a property for less than market value to his sister, he is entitled to do, but such loss must not be visited onto the wife. I do not accept the husband’s submission that there should not be an add back due to the length of time since the transaction occurred. There was no evidence of this purported agreement with Ms TT and she was not called give to evidence. There is also no evidence adduced as to the $100,000 the husband alleges he received. It was said to have been used for holidays, groceries and renovations costs of the B Street property. No sufficient evidence was produced to establish this other than the husband’s say so, which I do not accept without corroboration. The sum of $397,000 ought to be added back into the balance sheet. Although the transaction concerned occurred in 2019, that of itself does not mean that the Court ought to consider whether the sum ought to be added (Boulton & Boulton [2024] FedCFamC1A 132; (2024) FLC 94-202 at [33], [37] and [38]).

AA Street

In relation to the AA Street property, this was purchased in 2003 and sold in 2017 for $795,000. The property was retrospectively valued at $800,000. It was sold to Ms M, who is said to be a different person to the husband’s sister. Notwithstanding, the husband’s sister, Ms BB, used a power of attorney to sign on behalf of the husband to facilitate the sale. The wife submits that there is no proof of the funds being received and going towards the parties. The husband contends that it was applied to the purchase of the 3 L Street property. That contention has not been established by the admissible evidence. The sum of $308,493.06 ought be added back. The basis for the sum represents the net proceeds of sale which have not been accounted for by the husband.

Home Loan Redraws

As to the add back sought by the husband, whilst the wife does not contest that she used the funds for legal fees, she states that part of the funds was also used for living expenses. The husband conceded during closing submissions that he did not seek the entirety of the $124,000 added back, only that which was spent on legal fees. The wife could not say what amount she used towards her legal fees. Her evidence was that she had fees of $230,659, of which $76,000 was financed by a third party with the remainder being paid from the redraws and loans from friends and family. Otherwise, she says she used the funds to pay school fees which exceeded $124,000. In these circumstances, I find that 50 percent of the funds drawn are reasonably attributed to the payment of legal fees and the sum of $62,000 should be added back on account of this. I accept that half of the funds were used for household and school expenses.

CONTRIBUTIONS

Initial contributions

(c) interest in KK Street, Suburb LL.

By reason of my findings above, the husband also owned a 50 percent interest in the 1 L Street, 2 L Street and H Street properties as well as a one-third interest in the P Street property.
Otherwise, the parties entered into a contract of sale to jointly purchase the property at UU Street, Suburb NN. There was some contest about whether the wife received a loan from the maternal grandfather to assist the purchase of this property. The wife says she did whilst the maternal grandfather gave evidence that he did not, and that the funds came from the wife’s salary. Where the wife and husband both say that the wife received the loan from the maternal grandfather (albeit they dispute who repaid it), I find that the wife did receive a loan from the maternal grandfather. In any case, the husband overwhelmingly made the initial contributions.

Contributions during the course of the marriage

Throughout the marriage, the husband (some of which the wife says were unknown to her) had various dealings with properties This includes:
(a) in mid-2012, purchasing three of 10 shares in the property at VV Street, Suburb WW from the paternal grandfather. This property was sold in early 2014 but the husband denies he made any contributions to the property and it belonged to the paternal grandparents;

(b) in mid-2015, the husband purchased the B Street property for $860,000. The property was rented out as a rooming house and generated a monthly rental income of approximately $2,000. It is the husband’s evidence that he received loans from Ms BB and Mr K to assist in purchasing the property. These loans have not been proved;

(c) in about mid-2015, and as a result of my findings above, the husband purchased the D Street property for $1,405,000. It was briefly rented out before the husband began renovations on the property. The husband gives evidence that in 2021 he realised the property was operating at a significant loss;

(d) the husband purchased the G Street property in late 2015 for $1,185,000. As a result of my finding above, this was not done with any assistance from Ms O; and

(e) in late 2016, the husband purchased the 3 L Street property for $550,000. The husband gives evidence of difficulties associated with the property, although it was, at times, generating rental income. The property was sold to the husband’s nephew in 2022 for $450,000 (when the market value was $800,000), effectively wasting the sum of $350,000 which is taken into account as an addback.

In 2016, the parties also purchased the E Street property. The husband gives evidence that this was transferred to the wife’s name in 2019 due to the land tax associated with the property. The wife says she purchased the property. In any case, the E Street property is now in her sole name. As noted above, the husband sought an add back in respect of redraws on this property by the wife. As a result of my findings regarding the use of the redrawn funds, $62,000 should be added back.
Generally, the husband submits that he contributed significantly by way of renovations and maintenance of family assets. The husband was in full-time employment with U Pty Ltd until 2016 earning a relatively substantial income. He received a redundancy payment of $173,414. He thereafter worked on the parties’ properties, including renovating and managing the AirBnBs. Whilst this is not disputed by the wife, and in fact she agrees that the husband worked hard and applied his income for the benefit of the family, she asserts that her income was applied to the running of the household whilst the husband’s income was applied to accumulation and maintenance of properties. The wife submits that the husband retained the rental income and put that towards mortgages and other expense relating to their properties. The wife was employed as an educator on a full-time basis until early 2013, then commenced part-time from 2017-2018 and then full-time again from 2020. Moreover, from 2012 until COVID-19, she worked as a private tutor on the weekends.
The wife submits that she largely performed the homemaker duties, including caring for the children during the marriage and that the husband only undertook a minimal role with, at times, the assistance of the maternal and paternal grandparents. The husband contends that he was the primary carer of the children from 2020 when the wife resumed full-time work until separation. The wife’s evidence is that the parties engaged the assistance of a family friend which was provided for by NDIS funding but agreed that the husband did care for several of the children each Sunday. I find that the wife made the more significant contributions as a homemaker and carer for the children under what she describes in her affidavit evidence as difficult circumstances given the high needs of the children and the conduct of the husband.

Post-separation contributions

The wife has remained the primary carer of the children following separation. She is still employed on a full-time basis. The husband commenced paying child support 13 months following separation. His child support payments are currently assessed at $177 per month with the husband being $47,428.08 in arrears. The wife meets the children’s private school fees.

Kennon

The wife seeks to advance that pursuant to the principle established in Kennon v Kennon [1997] FamCA 27; (1997) FLC 92-757, that her contributions were made more arduous by family violence perpetrated onto her by the husband. In Dajani & Dajani [2025] FedCFamC1A 28; (2025) FLC 94-239, the Full Court said:
For the reasons given, the words “significantly” and “more arduous” are not to be read as coterminous with “exceptional”. Rather, they arise from the basis of the principle itself which focuses on contributions. If the nature and extent of a person’s contributions are made more difficult or harder so that they should be accorded greater weight, such that they should be taken into account in the determining of the outcome, they have therefore been “significantly impacted” or made “more arduous”. The focus is not on the conduct per se, but on its effects on contributions.
...
More recent cases have softened some of the harshness of the original application of the principles identified in Kennon. For example, it is now the position that the adverse effect of the violence on the contributions of a party can be inferred from the lay evidence of the parties and that there is no need to call evidence to “quantify” that effect.
It seems to me that regarding Kennon claims as “special” or “exceptional” is apt to mislead. In reality, all the majority said in that case was that a person’s contributions are to be assessed in the light of all of the circumstances and where those circumstances have the effect of making the contributions more difficult, onerous or arduous, that should be recognised in the assessment of contributions. That, of course, takes place in a holistic manner.
The wife puts her case on two bases, family violence by the husband onto her and the children which meant the wife could not leave them unsupervised with the husband and the conduct of the husband in these proceedings which were said to be a form of financial abuse by reason of a lack of disclosure and his conduct which made it difficult for the wife to prosecute her case.
The wife gives evidence of the husband’s alcohol use, saying that this increased his aggressiveness and her and the children were forced to act cautiously where his behaviour could change quickly.[67] The wife also gives evidence of verbal abuse by the husband where he would denigrate the wife and break things in the house if she raised her voice.[68] The wife tendered an audio call she made to Victoria Police regarding one of these incidents[69] as well as photos from 2011 and 2015 depicting smashed glass and plates on the floor.[70] The wife provides more detail about these particular incidents at paragraph 100 of her affidavit.
The wife further submits that the husband has physically assaulted the children by physically disciplining them. She gives extensive evidence about this at paragraph 100 of her affidavit and tendered photos of the children with either bruises or red marks.[71]
As to the financial abuse, senior counsel for the wife submitted during closing submissions that the husband’s behaviour has been calculated to “financially ruin” the wife. This includes the husband providing over 1,400 pages of largely undisclosed documents the day prior to the final hearing, including his debt with the ATO. Senior counsel for the wife contended that not only did this make it difficult for the wife to prosecute her case but also compromised any possibility of settlement where she was unaware of the extent of the parties’ financial situation. The wife also points towards various court applications made by the husband which she states were to exhaust her and her funding. This included objecting to subpoenas filed by the wife seeking financial documents in circumstances where he had failed to comply with his duty of disclosure.
The husband, whilst denying the claims set out above, submits that they do not give rise to a Kennon claim. The husband states that the family violence referred to are general statements and lack specificity. As to disclosure, the husband contends that even if the Court were to accept that there has been non-disclosure by the husband, it does not reach the level of deliberate non-disclosure.
I find that the wife’s contributions were made more onerous because of the husband’s conduct towards the wife and the children during the marriage and because of his conduct following separation in relation to the financial affairs of the parties and the conduct of this litigation. The wife’s evidence in relation to the husband’s conduct through the marriage was compelling and I accept that evidence. He has embarked on a complex and opaque series of transactions with members of his family for the purposes of seeking to diminish the family assets and to make the task of determining the assets more difficult and has made the wife contributions more onerous. I also find that the husband’s conduct in the course of these proceedings particularly in relation to the failure to give proper disclosure and the institution of numerous applications would have made the wife’s contributions as a carer of the children more difficult as she has been occupied in unnecessarily prolonged litigation.

Assessment of the contributions

The husband’s contribution to the acquisition of assets is significant as is the wife’s contribution financially since she commenced full time work. I accept the wife’s evidence that she had made more substantial contributions to the care of the children and as a home maker. I also find that the contributions that that the wife has made have been made more onerous because of the conduct of the husband throughout the course of the relationship. Taking those matters into account, I assess the contributions as equal.

SECTION 75(2) FACTORS

Subsection (2)(a) – the age and state of health of each of the parties

The wife is aged 44 years and is currently in good health. The husband is aged 46 years and is has been diagnosed with Hepatitis B since birth and has endured severe chronic psoriasis for over 25 years. He receives bimonthly injections and his liver must be closely monitored.

Subsection (2)(b) – the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment

The wife continues to work as an educator where she earns $116,000 per annum. The husband, despite his qualifications and experience, has commenced as a first-year apprentice earning $47,000 per annum. There is nothing of significant to suggest that either party has lacks capacity for employment.

Subsection (2)(c) – whether either party has the care or control of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years

By way of the final parenting orders made by consent, the wife will continue to have the primary care of the four children. The evidence was that the husband is in arrears of child support payments and the ongoing financial needs and commitments for the wife are considerable and will continue to be so.

Subsections (2)(d) and (e) – commitments of each of the parties that are necessary to enable the party to support himself or herself, and a child or another person that the party has a duty to maintain; and the responsibilities of either party to support any other person

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(f) – the eligibility of either party for a pension, allowance or benefit under any law of the Commonwealth, of a State or Territory or of another country; or any superannuation fund or scheme, whether the fund or scheme was established, or operates, within or outside Australia; and the rate of any such pension, allowance or benefit being paid to either party

The wife will have the benefit of NDIS funding and assistance for X and Y. By way of her Financial Statement filed 31 December 2024, she also receives $153 weekly for carers allowance and $67 for child disability assistance payment.

Subsection (2)(g) – where the parties have separated or divorced, a standard of living that in all the circumstances is reasonable

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(h) – the extent to which the payment of maintenance to the party whose maintenance is under consideration would increase the earning capacity of that party by enabling that party to undertake a course of education or training or to establish himself or herself in a business or otherwise to obtain an adequate income

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(ha) – the effect of any proposed order on the ability of a creditor of a party to recover the creditor’s debt, so far as that effect is relevant

The effect of the orders on the creditors is addressed above.

Subsection (2)(j) – the extent to which the party whose maintenance is under consideration has contributed to the income, earning capacity, property and financial resources of the other party

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(k) – the duration of the marriage and the extent to which it has affected the earning capacity of the party whose maintenance is under consideration

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(l) – the need to protect a party who wishes to continue that party’s role as a parent

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(m) – if either party is cohabiting with another person—the financial circumstances relating to the cohabitation

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(n) – the terms of any order made or proposed to be made under section 79 in relation to the property of the parties; or vested bankruptcy property in relation to a bankrupt party

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(naa) – the terms of any order or declaration made, or proposed to be made, under Part VIIIAB in relation to a party to the marriage; or a person who is a party to a de facto relationship with a party to the marriage; or the property of or vested bankruptcy property in relation to a person covered by the categories aforementioned

This is not a relevant consideration.

Subsection (2)(na) – any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the marriage has provided, is to provide, or might be liable to provide in the future, for a child of the marriage

As noted above, child support payments are currently assessed at $177 per month with the husband being $47,428.08 in arrears.[72]

Subsection (2)(o) – any fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account

Given the conduct of the husband through the course of these proceedings, his conduct in failing to pay adequate child support and adoption of a position in relation to the financial position of the wife post separation means that it is likely that the wife will bear a more substantial share of the ongoing responsibilities for the physical, emotional and financial care of the four children of the marriage. These are factors which I take into account.

Subsections (2)(p) and (q) – the terms of any financial agreement and any Part VIIIAB financial agreement that is binding on the parties to the marriage

This is not a relevant consideration.

Assessment of s 75(2) factors

Given these matters, I assess an allowance of 15 per cent in favour of the wife and I find that a just and equitable orders for the distribution of the property of the parties is 65 per cent in favour of the wife. The allowance takes into account the significantly greater financial burden that the wife will have as a result of the primary care of the children and the husband’s present failure to adequately financially support his children.

CONCLUSION

The orders make provision for the wife to be trustee for sale for any property that is required to be sold. Having regard to the corporation between the parties and events post separation regarding the sale of properties, the Court has no confidence that the husband will cooperate with the orders.
To give effect to this split, the wife will receive the E Street property, the proceeds of sale from the D Street property, the balance of the funds in trust after the sale of the G Street property and the proceeds of sale from the B Street property. This totals $4,279,401.2. The E Street, D Street and B Street properties are encumbered in the amount of $2,402,098. The orders also make provision for the wife to pay $10,000 for the joint liability relating to the parties NAB account and the liability owing to the ATO to the extent of $301,918.90 from the proceeds of the sale of the D Street property. This leaves the wife with $1,565,384.3. The wife will have a further $62,000 by reason of the add backs for the home loan redraws. This leaves the wife with $1,627,384.30 meaning that to achieve a result which reflects a split of 65 per cent in her favour, the husband is required to make a payment of about $719,234.88 to the wife (not taking into account selling costs and the like).
The husband will retain his interest in the H Street, P Street, 2 L Street and 1 L Street properties. Taking into account their respective mortgages, this leaves the husband with $818,720. Taking into consideration the addbacks which total $1,165,521.06, this leaves the husband with $1,984,241.06. The husband must pay the land tax liability in the sum of $1,442 and $719,234.88 to the wife. This will result in the husband receiving $1,263,564.18. Of this amount, $1,165,521.06 is the assets notionally added back. The husband also has to make payment of the ATO penalties, although that amount, by reason of his conduct, has been excluded from the balance sheet.

Costs

The wife sought orders that the husband pay costs in respect of several interlocutory orders where costs had been reserved. I will deal with those matters in any costs applications made in respect of the proceedings. At present, there is insufficient evidence in relation to the quantum sought and why the costs orders should be made in favour of the wife.
